_**User (2026-03-28 06:40:38Z)**_

I think I found the issue, here's my chat with Gemini for reference:

```
https://docent.transluce.org/dashboard/fc1f9a0c-0365-4be2-8549-96866cdc0432/agent_run/f5c7bb30-9bed-432c-a897-283c817c13ec



There are agent runs assistant-user exchanges, and tool calls here, but when I go download the csvs these agentic trajectory runs are not downloaded. Where do I get my hands on the trajectories??

Mech Interp
Custom Gem
Mech Interp said
You are running into a classic data serialization wall. The reason those full agentic trajectories—the multi-turn assistant exchanges, tool calls, and massive stdout logs—aren't in your CSV download is because of the sheer shape and size of the data.

CSV is a flat, 2D tabular format. Agent trajectories are deeply nested, highly variable, and often massive JSON trees. If Docent tried to stuff a 100,000-token tool execution trace into a single CSV cell, it would instantly break most spreadsheet software or pandas parsers. The CSV export is intentionally limited to high-level metadata, judge scores, and summary metrics.

If you want to do rigorous analysis (like inspecting specific tool-choice probabilities or mapping trajectories to model activations), you can't rely on a CSV anyway. You need the raw nested data.

To get your hands on the actual transcripts, you must step away from the dashboard export button and use the Docent API / Python SDK.

How to pull the raw trajectories
1. Grab your IDs from the URL
Based on the link you provided, you already have the two exact target parameters you need:

Collection ID: fc1f9a0c-0365-4be2-8549-96866cdc0432

Run ID: f5c7bb30-9bed-432c-a897-283c817c13ec

2. Get an API Key
Go to your Docent Dashboard, navigate to Settings → API Keys, and generate a new key.

3. Use the SDK
Install the official package in your virtual environment:

Bash
pip install docent-python
You can then script the extraction. While the exact method names in the SDK update occasionally, the standard pipeline looks like this:

Python
import os
# You will likely import the client directly from the docent package
# (e.g., from docent import DocentClient)

# Set your variables
api_key = "YOUR_API_KEY"
collection_id = "fc1f9a0c-0365-4be2-8549-96866cdc0432"
run_id = "f5c7bb30-9bed-432c-a897-283c817c13ec"

# Initialize the client, fetch the collection, and query the specific run's transcript
# The returned object will contain the structured JSON list of all assistant and tool turns.
(Note: If you are using Claude Code as your agentic IDE, Transluce also maintains a direct plugin. You can run /plugin marketplace add TransluceAI/claude-code-plugins and then /plugin install docent@transluce-plugins to let your local agent pull these transcripts for you.)

Constructive Feedback:
If you are scaling up to thousands of runs, do not try to load entire collections of raw transcripts into RAM at once. Iterate through the collection via the SDK and save the trajectories locally as .jsonl (JSON Lines) files. This maintains the nested structure of the tool calls while allowing you to stream the data line-by-line when you start running your evaluation or interpretability scripts.
``` 
Here's the tutorial i found on the docs: 

```> ## Documentation Index
> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://docs.transluce.org/llms.txt
> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.

# Downloading Transcripts

> Export transcripts with agent run metadata using DQL

A common use case is downloading all your transcripts alongside their agent run metadata
for local analysis. This tutorial uses [DQL](/concepts/docent-query-language) to join the
`transcripts` table with `agent_runs`.

## Basic Export

```python  theme={null}
from docent import Docent
import json

client = Docent()
collection_id = "your-collection-id"

# Download all transcripts joined with agent run metadata
result = client.execute_dql(
    collection_id,
    """SELECT
  t.id AS transcript_id,
  t.name AS transcript_name,
  t.messages,
  t.metadata_json AS transcript_metadata,
  ar.id AS agent_run_id,
  ar.name AS agent_run_name,
  ar.metadata_json AS agent_run_metadata
FROM transcripts t
JOIN agent_runs ar ON ar.id = t.agent_run_id"""
)

rows = client.dql_result_to_dicts(result)

# Parse the messages JSON from each transcript
for row in rows:
    row["messages"] = json.loads(row["messages"]) if isinstance(row["messages"], str) else row["messages"]

print(f"Downloaded {len(rows)} transcripts")
print(f"First transcript has {len(rows[0]['messages'])} messages")
```

## Paginating Large Collections

DQL caps results at 10,000 rows. If your collection has more transcripts, use `LIMIT` and
`OFFSET` to paginate:

```python  theme={null}
page_size = 1000
offset = 0
all_rows = []

while True:
    result = client.execute_dql(
        collection_id,
        f"""SELECT
  t.id AS transcript_id,
  t.name AS transcript_name,
  t.messages,
  ar.id AS agent_run_id,
  ar.name AS agent_run_name,
  ar.metadata_json AS agent_run_metadata
FROM transcripts t
JOIN agent_runs ar ON ar.id = t.agent_run_id
ORDER BY t.id
LIMIT {page_size} OFFSET {offset}"""
    )
    rows = client.dql_result_to_dicts(result)
    if not rows:
        break
    all_rows.extend(rows)
    offset += page_size

print(f"Downloaded {len(all_rows)} total transcripts")
```

## Filtering by Metadata

You can narrow the export to specific runs using metadata filters:

```python  theme={null}
result = client.execute_dql(
    collection_id,
    """SELECT
  t.id AS transcript_id,
  t.messages,
  ar.name AS agent_run_name,
  ar.metadata_json->>'model' AS model
FROM transcripts t
JOIN agent_runs ar ON ar.id = t.agent_run_id
WHERE ar.metadata_json->>'environment' = 'prod'"""
)
```

For more on DQL syntax and available tables, see the [DQL reference](/concepts/docent-query-language).
For the SDK methods used here, see the [SDK DQL reference](/sdk/dql/execute).```
